name: ce-db
currentState: ce-db is a clean, app-tier Rust SDK (edition 2024) that reimagines Firestore by composing CE primitives: ce-coord `Merged` (a multi-writer CRDT union-of-logs) for the document store, ce-cap for capability gating, and ce-coord `Snapshot` for compaction. The architecture is genuinely sound and the pure model works: `DbMachine` (src/doc.rs) is a deterministic, order-independent field-level LWW merge machine with whole-doc Set, field-level Patch (null = field delete), and Delete tombstones with resurrection; the query engine (src/query.rs) does conjunctive top-level field filters + order + limit; the access layer (src/access.rs) wraps ce-cap minting/attenuation/verification. Convergence, query semantics, capability attenuation, and serde of large numbers are all proven by strong unit + proptest + edge-case suites, plus a real two-node ephemeral-mesh live integration suite (tests/live.rs, tests/realtime_sync.rs) that gracefully skips when the ce binary is absent. No `todo!()`/`unimplemented!()`/`panic!` in production paths; the only `println!` are in the CLI binary (allowed). The big honesty gap: the capability layer is a self-contained module never wired into `Collection` read/write paths (no enforcement point), reads do a full O(n) re-fold of the entire op union on every call with no materialized cache, and "realtime watch" actually requires an external poll loop because `watch` only fires when something calls `pull()`.
plan: Wire capability enforcement into the data path: add a guarded Collection (or an auth policy passed to open) that calls CollectionGrant::verify for db:read on get/query/watch and db:write on set/patch/delete, against configured accepted roots and an on-chain is_revoked closure. Add integration tests proving unauthorized reads/writes are denied. This closes the single largest credibility gap vs Firestore.

Add a materialized document cache inside Collection invalidated by the watch counter, so get/query/len are not full O(n) re-folds of the whole op history on every call. Benchmark before/after on a 10k-op collection.

Make realtime push-based: have ce-coord (or a Collection-owned background task) call pull() and fire the watch when peer ops arrive, so next_change delivers peer changes without an external poller. Then add an e2e test that asserts a peer write wakes a watcher with no manual refresh.

Implement per-document change deltas (added/modified/removed, i.e. Firestore DocumentChange) and a query-scoped/doc-scoped listener API on top of snapshots, so onSnapshot semantics are real, not whole-collection diffs.
Add query cursors (startAt/startAfter/endAt/endBefore + offset) and multi-field order_by with doc-id tie-break, enabling real pagination over ordered results.

Add input validation and DoS limits: max document byte size, max field count, max collection op-count/peer-count, validated collection/doc-id charset and length, returning anyhow errors on violation. Add failure-path tests.
Implement subcollections: hierarchical addressing (collection/doc/sub/doc), per-subcollection Merged instances, and collection-group queries. This is core Firestore data modeling.

Add transactions and batched writes: a WriteBatch that proposes multiple DocOps atomically into the writer log, and a runTransaction optimistic read-then-write helper (documenting the CRDT caveat that cross-device atomicity still needs the chain).

Fix integer-precision in ordering/comparison (compare i64/u64 numerically instead of routing through as_f64), and harden the Lamport allocator to a single critical section / parking_lot mutex to remove lock().unwrap() and the two-lock race.
Add richer operators (array-contains-any, in, not-in) and nested field-path support for both queries and Patch field-merge (dotted paths), so nested objects merge field-by-field like Firestore.
Add FieldValue-style atomic ops (serverTimestamp, increment, arrayUnion/arrayRemove) as new DocOp kinds with CRDT-friendly merge semantics (e.g. a PN-counter for increment).
Improve compaction: GC tombstones and superseded patch ops across the merged set (not just the local writer log), and add a snapshot+tail == full-replay equivalence test at the ce-db level.

Documentation pass: add examples/, correct the README claims about enforcement/realtime, fix the stale tests/ layout list, add an architecture doc covering the cost model and CRDT-vs-consensus boundary, and document supported query shapes and limits.

featureGaps.notes: CollectionGrant::verify exists and is well-tested but Collection::set/patch/delete/get/query never call it. There is no enforcement point: any holder of a Coord can read/write any collection. Firestore security rules actually gate every operation. Needs a guarded Collection wrapper that verifies db:read/db:write/db:admin per op against accepted roots + on-chain revocation.

Firestore's core hierarchical model (collection/doc/subcollection/doc...) is absent. DocPath keeps a slash-containing doc_id but there is no subcollection addressing, no collection-group queries, no nested document tree. Only flat collections exist.
No startAt/startAfter/endAt/endBefore/offset. Only limit (truncate). Firestore pagination over ordered results is impossible; a UI cannot page through large collections.

No runTransaction (read-then-conditional-write with optimistic concurrency) and no WriteBatch (atomic multi-doc commit). README correctly punts uniqueness/balances to the chain, but Firestore-style transactional multi-doc commits are a headline feature that is entirely missing.

watch/next_change only deliver a whole-collection Snapshot on any change. There is no per-document added/modified/removed delta stream (Firestore's DocumentChange), no doc-scoped listener, no query-scoped listener. A UI must diff full snapshots itself.

Every query is a full O(n) scan of the folded map (query.rs run). No single-field or composite indexes, no index-backed range scans. README admits this but Firestore's defining performance trait (indexed queries) is absent, so large collections are linear.

Missing array-contains-any, in, not-in, !=-with-index, and OR queries. Only Eq/Ne/Gt/Ge/Lt/Le/Contains on AND-ed top-level fields. order_by supports a single field only (no multi-field sort, no order on missing-tie-break by doc id).

Filters and order_by operate only on top-level keys (doc.get(field)). Firestore supports dotted field paths (a.b.c) for both queries and patch updates; ce-db Patch also only merges top-level fields, so nested objects are wholesale-replaced, not field-merged.

No FieldValue.serverTimestamp(), increment(), arrayUnion/arrayRemove. These are common Firestore write primitives; here every write is a literal value, so counters race and concurrent array edits clobber.

Collection state lives only in ce-coord's in-memory union plus the writer log; there is no local on-disk document cache for fast cold start independent of replaying the log, and no explicit offline persistence layer beyond what ce-coord provides. Compact is per-device only and does not GC tombstones across the merged set.
No way to enumerate collections, get doc create/update times, or read a document's version/metadata (Firestore exposes updateTime, createTime, readTime). Snapshot.op_count is the only coarse version.

robustnessIssues.issue: Capability gating is defined but never enforced on any data operation. Collection::set/patch/delete/get/query (src/collection.rs:110-158) perform reads/writes with zero authorization check; CollectionGrant::verify (src/access.rs:169) is only exercised by tests and the CLI grant/inspect commands. The 'security rules / IAM' row of the README table is aspirational at the call sites.

No bounds/limits on document size, field count, value size, op count, or number of followed peers. A single huge JSON document or an unbounded number of ops/writers will grow the in-memory union unboundedly (every read re-folds it). No validation that a doc_id or collection name is well-formed/length-bounded. This is a DoS / OOM surface with no backpressure.

Every read/get/query/len calls merged.read which calls pull() then a full O(n) fold of the entire op union (ce-coord merged.rs:253). There is no materialized/cached document map invalidated on change, so read cost grows linearly with total history (not live doc count). For a long-lived collection this degrades badly even after compaction.

Realtime is poll-driven, not push. watch() only fires when pull() runs, and pull() runs only inside read/propose/op_count. Peer ops arriving over the mesh do not wake a watcher until something calls refresh()/read(); the live test confirms this by looping with refresh() + 300ms sleep. next_change therefore cannot deliver peer changes without an external poller, contradicting the 'onSnapshot listeners' framing.

Lamport clock uses two separate lock acquisitions (advance_clock_past_merged then next_key both lock self.lamport) with a read of merged_ops between them, so concurrent writers on the same Collection can interleave and produce a non-strictly-increasing local sequence under contention; the (lamport,writer) key still won't collide across devices but same-device concurrency is not airtight.

lock().unwrap() on the Lamport mutex in production write paths will panic the task if the mutex is poisoned (e.g. a panic while holding it elsewhere). Standards forbid unwrap in prod paths; should be a lock-poison-tolerant access or parking_lot mutex.

compact() snapshots only THIS device's writer log; it does not compact peer logs or garbage-collect tombstones from the merged union, so deleted documents' tombstones and old patch ops persist forever in the folded set, and a fresh follower of a peer still replays that peer's full log.

Query order() with f64 total_cmp coerces all JSON numbers through as_f64 (query.rs:159), losing precision for i64/u64 values beyond 2^53 in comparisons/ordering even though the values round-trip exactly in storage. A large-integer field will sort/compare incorrectly.

CLI parse_filter swallows JSON parse errors and silently falls back to treating the value as a bare string (bin/ce_db.rs:245-246), so a typo like age:gt:3O becomes a string compare that silently returns nothing rather than erroring.

testGaps.area: Capability enforcement at the data layer (there is none, so there is nothing to test) — once a guarded Collection exists, needs read/write denial tests
Concurrent same-device writes hammering next_key/advance_clock to prove monotonic local Lamport allocation under contention
Document/field/op-count size limits and rejection of oversized inputs (no limits exist, so no tests)
Realtime peer-change delivery WITHOUT an external poll loop — current live test masks the poll dependency with refresh()+sleep; needs a test asserting watch wakes on a peer op
Compaction correctness: snapshot+tail == full replay at the ce-db level (only asserted that a checkpoint is produced, not that a bootstrapped reader matches a full-replay reader)
Multi-writer (>2) convergence and writer-churn (add_writer mid-stream) over the live mesh
Query ordering precision for integers beyond 2^53 (proptest currently bounds vals to 0..1000)
CLI argument parsing and error paths (no tests for bin/ce_db.rs build_query/parse_filter/grant/inspect)
Lock-poisoning behavior of the Lamport mutex

docGaps: No examples/ directory; the only runnable example is the doctest in lib.rs and the CLI. A worked end-to-end example (two collections, capability handoff, snapshot bootstrap) would help.

README and rustdoc claim 'security rules / IAM' and 'onSnapshot realtime listeners' as delivered, but capability checks are not enforced on data ops and realtime is poll-driven — the docs should be explicit that CollectionGrant is a verification toolkit not an enforcement layer, and that watch needs a poller.
No architecture/design doc covering the O(n)-fold-per-read cost model, unbounded log growth, and the CRDT-vs-consensus boundary beyond the brief README 'What ce-db is not' section.
No CHANGELOG, no documented limits (max doc size, max collection size, supported query shapes), no MSRV note.
Layout section of README lists only realtime_sync.rs under tests/ but four test files exist (edge_cases, prop_db, live, realtime_sync) — stale.
No doctest/example for compaction or for following peers via add_writer.

name: ce-query
currentState: ce-query is a BigQuery-shaped distributed map-reduce engine over CE content-addressed blob datasets, built as an app on the ce-rs SDK (no node changes). The pure core is genuinely strong and real, not stubbed: NDJSON dataset sharding (dataset.rs), a filter-aggregate-group query model with a pure deterministic map step (query.rs), a monoid combiner algebra for the reduce with associativity/commutativity/identity (combine.rs), rendezvous-hash (HRW) shard→host placement with deterministic failover ranking (plan.rs), a distributed executor with retry/redistribute-on-drop over a MapHost trait seam (engine.rs), two real MapHost impls — coordinator-local fetch and true mesh request/reply — plus an on-host serve_map agent (mesh.rs), a hand-written SQL-ish parser covering SELECT/WHERE/GROUP BY/ORDER BY/LIMIT (sql.rs), an in-memory hash-join + co-partitioned distributed_join (join.rs), ce-cap dataset-scoped query:read tokens (caps.rs), and an atomic JSON catalog (catalog.rs). Production paths are clean of unwrap/expect/panic/todo (the only non-test .expect is a provably-safe key lookup in main.rs:175). Testing is unusually good for this stage: ~109 unit tests plus proptests proving the algebra laws, shard-count invariance, JSON round-trips, HRW balance/re-homing, order/limit, join cardinality, and engine failure-injection (dead hosts, transient 5xx, missing blobs, attempt caps). The gaps vs real BigQuery are scope, not soundness: no columnar/predicate-pushdown/projection, joins are not wired into SQL or the distributed engine (run only in one process), the engine fans shards sequentially (await per shard) rather than concurrently, there is no redundant/quorum execution for result verification, and revocation is hardcoded off in the serve agent.
plan: Make engine::run fan shards concurrently: replace the sequential for-loop (engine.rs:125) with a bounded FuturesUnordered/JoinSet (configurable max in-flight), preserving the per-shard retry/redistribute walk; add an overall query deadline and a per-attempt timeout. Add concurrency tests asserting out-of-order partial arrival still reduces correctly.

Add redundant/quorum execution for result verification: optionally map each shard on K hosts (K from RunConfig), compare the K Partials, and require agreement (or majority); surface a HostDisagreement error. Test with a mock host that returns a corrupted Partial — this is the missing 'verification via redundancy' BigQuery analog and the strongest trust win on an open mesh.

Fix numeric correctness: add integer SUM/COUNT accumulators (i128 base units per the money convention) and use a deterministic summation (e.g. Kahan or sorted/pairwise) for f64 AVG/SUM; extend the associativity/commutativity proptests to fractional and large-magnitude inputs so they actually exercise the float path.

Support projection (non-aggregate) queries: relax Query::validate (query.rs:275), add a Project/columns path to map_shard and the SQL parser (`SELECT a, b FROM t WHERE ...`), with LIMIT and a result-row cap. This unlocks the single most common BigQuery query shape.

Add predicate/projection pushdown to the shard scan: have serve_map/LocalMapHost apply the WHERE predicate and project only needed columns while decoding (mesh.rs), and add per-shard min/max column stats to Shard so the planner can skip shards that cannot match (partition/skip pruning). This is BigQuery's core efficiency story.

Add query cost limits: estimate bytes/rows to scan from Dataset/Shard counts, enforce a max-bytes/max-rows/max-groups budget in engine::run, return a clear LimitExceeded error before dispatch, and wire per-query credit pricing through the CE economy. Prevents unbounded memory and spend (DoS).

Wire JOIN through SQL and the distributed engine: extend sql.rs to parse `JOIN ... ON`, and make engine::run execute co-partitioned joins across hosts (each host owns matching left/right buckets) feeding into the aggregate map — turning join.rs from in-process reference semantics into a real distributed operator.

Harden the serve agent: consult on-chain revocation (replace `|_,_| false` at main.rs:338 with a real revocation lookup), load accepted_roots and self_tags from config, spawn each map request concurrently to remove head-of-line blocking, and cap inbound request size/rate.

Replace string-substring error classification (mesh.rs:148-169) with typed SDK errors from ce-rs (or a structured error enum the SDK exposes), so MissingBlob/Timeout/HostError are determined reliably.

Add a catalog write lock (advisory file lock or atomic compare-and-swap on a version field) so concurrent `dataset add` cannot clobber, plus dataset append (add shards to an existing dataset) and a dataset version/generation field for time-travel.
Add streaming/incremental reduce (fold partials as they arrive rather than buffering a Vec) and pagination for large group sets; add memory-bounds tests for high-cardinality GROUP BY.

Documentation and examples: ship examples/ with a sample sales.ndjson and a runnable quickstart, fix README doctests to compile, add an honest 'vs BigQuery' limitations matrix, and document the f64 precision and revocation caveats.

featureGaps.notes: engine::run (engine.rs:125) processes shards in a sequential `for task ... { host.map(...).await }` loop, so the headline 'fan map tasks across hosts' is actually serialized — wall-clock scales with shard count, not host count. Needs FuturesUnordered/JoinSet with a bounded concurrency limit.

Shards are whole-row NDJSON fetched and fully decoded on every query (mesh.rs serve_map/LocalMapHost). No column pruning, no partition pruning, no skip-on-stats. BigQuery's core value (scan only needed columns/partitions) is absent; the whole shard is always materialized.
Query::validate (query.rs:275) rejects any query with zero aggregates: `SELECT a, b FROM t WHERE ...` (non-aggregate projection) is unsupported. BigQuery's most common query shape cannot run.

featureGaps.feature: JOIN in SQL and across the mesh
featureGaps.notes: join.rs hash_join/distributed_join exist and are tested, but are never reachable from sql::parse (explicitly out of scope) nor from engine::run — distributed_join still executes in one process over fully-materialized row vecs, so cross-dataset joins do not actually distribute or stream.

plan.rs assigns each shard to exactly one primary host; on success the result is trusted. There is no N-way redundant map + agreement/quorum check, so a single malicious or buggy host silently corrupts an aggregate. This is the explicit 'result verification via redundancy' reference feature and is missing.

No bytes-scanned estimate, no max-bytes-billed guard, no row/shard/time budget, no per-query credit pricing via the CE economy. RunReport tracks attempts only. A query over a huge dataset has no cost ceiling (DoS / unbounded spend).
sql.rs supports only COUNT(*)/SUM/MIN/MAX/AVG of a bare column, comparison-only WHERE, no arithmetic/functions in SELECT or WHERE, no HAVING, no DISTINCT, no nested queries.

All aggregates coerce to f64 (query.rs as_f64, Accum::Sum(f64)). SUM over large counts or money loses precision and is non-deterministic across reorderings at the ULP level — violates the project's integer-base-unit money convention. No integer SUM or decimal type.

Partials are collected into a Vec then reduced once (engine.rs:168); results and shards are fully buffered in memory. No streaming reduce-as-arrive (despite the monoid supporting it) and no pagination of large group sets.

Dataset.schema is informational only (dataset.rs); rows are schema-on-read. No type validation, no column existence checks against the query, no error on typo'd ORDER BY/GROUP BY columns (silently no-op).

featureGaps.feature: Capability revocation enforcement in the serving agent
featureGaps.notes: serve_cmd (main.rs:338) passes is_revoked as `&|_, _| false`, so on-chain RevokeCapability is never consulted by the map agent — a revoked-but-unexpired token still authorizes queries. Also accepted_roots and self_tags are hardcoded empty.
dataset add fully replaces; no incremental append of shards, no row deletion, no dataset versioning/time-travel (a BigQuery staple).

robustnessIssues.issue: Sequential shard execution under load: engine::run awaits each shard's map serially, so a single slow/timing-out host stalls the whole query for its 30s timeout before the next shard even starts. Combined with no overall query deadline this is a latency/DoS hazard.

No upper bound on dataset size, shard count, result-group cardinality, or per-shard byte size in engine/serve_map. A GROUP BY on a high-cardinality column or a giant shard materializes unbounded memory on both coordinator and serving host (decode_shard returns the whole Vec<Row>). No max-bytes/rows guard.

Error classification by substring-matching error text (contains '404'/'timeout'/'504'). Fragile: SDK wording changes or a body legitimately containing '404' misclassifies MissingBlob vs Timeout vs HostError, changing retry behavior. Should classify on a typed SDK error, not strings.

Map agent reply on encode failure is `serde_json::to_vec(&reply).unwrap_or_default()` — on (extremely unlikely) serialization failure it replies with empty bytes, which the coordinator then tries to deserialize as MapReply and fails as Corrupt; the requester gets a confusing error rather than a clear host error.

Catalog claims 'single-writer' and atomic save, but there is no file lock; two concurrent `ce-query dataset add` invocations race (load-modify-save) and the last writer silently clobbers the other's dataset. No advisory lock or compare-and-swap.

Revocation and roots are stubbed in the serving path: is_revoked is `|_,_| false` and accepted_roots/self_tags are empty, so capability revocation and delegated-root grants are silently non-functional in production despite caps.rs supporting them.

f64 aggregation is order-sensitive at the floating-point level; the monoid 'associativity/commutativity' proptests pass only because test inputs are small integers exactly representable in f64. Real-world SUM/AVG over many fractional values will give run-dependent results, undermining the determinism the design relies on for safe retry/redistribution.

serve loop polls every poll_ms and processes messages sequentially with an awaited get_object+map per request; a slow shard fetch blocks all other inbound map requests on that host (head-of-line blocking). No per-request spawn or concurrency.

No query-level timeout/deadline in engine::run; max_attempts_per_shard bounds host hops but a host that accepts and stalls under the (default 30s) per-request timeout multiplies across attempts and shards with no total cap.

testGaps.area: Concurrent/parallel fan-out behavior and bounded concurrency (once implemented); ordering of out-of-order partial arrival under real async
MeshMapHost end-to-end against a real or in-memory CeClient (request/reply round-trip, timeout, error mapping) — only Local/Mock are exercised; MeshMapHost::map has no test
serve_cmd agent loop: malformed payload, missing reply_token, unauthorized-with-grant, revoked token, multi-request handling — only serve_map's reject path is unit-tested, the loop itself is untested
Catalog concurrent writers (two dataset add racing) and crash-during-rename recovery
f64 aggregation precision/associativity over fractional and large-magnitude values (current proptests only use small integers, hiding the float non-associativity)
CLI end-to-end: dataset add → plan → run round-trip against a node (no e2e/integration test of the binary exists)
Result verification / quorum across redundant hosts (feature absent, so untested) — adversarial host returning a wrong Partial is not detected or tested
join.rs distributed/streaming over the engine (joins are never run through engine::run, so the distributed join path is untested end-to-end)
Large-result / high-cardinality GROUP BY memory bounds and LIMIT-on-huge-group-set behavior

docGaps: No rustdoc doctests beyond the one in lib.rs/README; README claims '1 doctest' but examples in README use `?` in non-async/non-Result context and `users`/`orders` undefined (the join snippet and the sql::parse snippet won't compile as doctests — they are illustrative only).
No CHANGELOG, CONTRIBUTING, or architecture decision record beyond inline module docs (which are, to their credit, excellent).
No examples/ directory and no sample .ndjson dataset, so a new user cannot run the CLI quickstart as written without inventing data.

README overstates maturity vs BigQuery (no mention that projection queries, JOIN-in-SQL, predicate pushdown, cost limits, and quorum verification are absent) — the limitations section only lists subqueries/HAVING.
No documentation of the f64 precision limitation for SUM/AVG, which matters given the project's integer-base-unit money convention.
serve/grant security caveats (revocation hardcoded off, roots empty) are undocumented — an operator would assume --require-grant honors revocation.

name: ce-fn
currentState: ce-fn is an app-tier Rust crate (edition 2024, ~1740 LOC across 6 source files) that composes existing CE primitives via the ce-rs SDK and ce-cap into a Cloud-Functions/Cloud-Run-style control plane. The architecture is clean and idiomatic: `function.rs` (Function/Handler/Deployment specs + JSON Registry), `placement.rs` (pure atlas-filter + reputation-rank host selection), `protocol.rs` (InvokeRequest/InvokeResponse/TriggerEvent wire types over hex+JSON), `caps.rs` (ce-cap grant/authorize wrappers), `client.rs` (FnClient: deploy/invoke/kill/run_trigger + a SeenWindow dedup), and a clap CLI in `src/bin/ce-fn.rs`. What genuinely works AND is testable offline: name validation, registry persistence, pure placement/ranking, protocol round-trips, and capability grant/authorize/revoke. All the SDK methods it calls (mesh_deploy, mesh_deploy_wasm, request, subscribe, messages, atlas, history.delivered_work, mesh_kill) exist in ce-rs. The decisive gap is that there is NO serve-side runtime anywhere in the workspace that answers `ce-fn/invoke` AppRequests — the README itself admits this ("That serve-side daemon is the sibling deliverable... ce-fn ships the client/control plane"). Consequently `invoke` and `on`/triggers cannot complete end-to-end against any real host (they would block until the 600s timeout). There are no todo!()/unimplemented!()/panic! in non-test paths and no unwrap() in production code — all unwrap/expect occur only in `#[cfg(test)]` and tests/. This is a well-written half (control plane only) of a two-half product.
plan: 1. Build the serve-side runtime (ce-fn-serve daemon, mirroring rdev serve): subscribe to ce-fn/invoke, on each AppRequest decode InvokeRequest, enforce caps via caps::authorize_invoke, run the named handler (exec into the already-deployed container cell or instantiate the WASM module), capture stdout/exit, and reply with InvokeResponse via CeClient::reply. Without this nothing in the crate works end-to-end. Ship it with at least one container and one WASM example handler.

2. Add an end-to-end integration test (gated, --ignored) that starts a local node + the runtime, deploys a trivial echo function, invokes it, asserts the round-trip, and kills it. This is the only test that exercises the product's reason for existing.

3. Make the trigger loop reliable: switch run_trigger from polling CeClient::messages to the SSE messages_stream, keep the SeenWindow dedup, and add bounded retry with backoff per invocation. Document at-least-once vs at-most-once and add a failure-path test for dropped/duplicate/malformed events.

4. Add env vars and secrets to Function (env: Vec<(String,String)>, secrets referencing a store), thread them through BidSpec/mesh_deploy and into the runtime's handler launch. This is table-stakes FaaS and currently entirely absent.

5. Introduce per-invocation billing or a payment-channel path: open a channel on deploy, sign a receipt per invoke in invoke_with, so the economics match FaaS pay-per-call rather than a held cell. At minimum document and expose invocation accounting.

6. Add `ce-fn logs` and basic observability: stream handler stdout/stderr, and surface invocation count/latency/error metrics (the runtime publishes them on a metrics topic or the registry records last-invoked/error counts).

7. Harden inputs and limits: cap InvokeRequest/InvokeResponse payload size (constant + validation on encode and decode), validate module_hash and host node-id as 64-hex in the deploy path, and reject oversized hex on decode to close the OOM/DoS vector.

8. Make placement resilient: rank top-N (not just best), and on deploy iterate candidates with retry/fallback when a host fails (handle the documented atlas TOCTOU). Parallelize the history fan-out with a timeout and stop treating fetch errors as zero reputation silently.

9. Make the Registry robust: atomic temp-file+rename writes, an advisory file lock against concurrent CLI processes, a schema version field with migration, and a `ce-fn forget <name> --force` to drop entries whose host is unreachable. Add a reconcile that checks actual host job state.
10. Add function versioning: key the registry by name+revision, support deploy creating a new revision, `ce-fn rollback`, and (optionally) traffic-split across revisions for canary.

11. Add richer trigger types: an HTTP-ingress trigger (public invoke URL via the node), a cron/scheduler trigger, and a structured CloudEvents mapping for TriggerEvent so storage/pubsub producers integrate cleanly.

12. Add concurrency/scale controls to Function (replicas, min/max instances, max-concurrency) and place multiple cells across the top-N hosts with client-side load balancing on invoke; expose scale-to-zero vs warm.

13. Fill test and doc gaps: property tests for protocol and placement, a normative serve protocol spec doc, an examples/ directory with runnable handlers, an architecture/lifecycle doc, and a clear 'data plane not included / Cloud-Run-style billing' banner at the top of the README.

featureGaps.notes: No daemon answers ce-fn/invoke AppRequests by running the deployed container/WASM and replying with InvokeResponse. Without it deploy places a cell but invoke/triggers never return output. This is THE blocker — the analog of GCF actually running your code.

GCF has min/max instances, max-concurrent-requests-per-instance, and scale-to-zero. ce-fn deploys exactly one cell on one host (deploy_on picks a single Candidate) with no replica count, no per-host concurrency cap, no horizontal scale-out, no scale-to-zero. No load distribution across multiple hosts for the same function.

No warm-pool, no pre-pull, no keep-alive vs scale-to-zero distinction. The 600s default invoke timeout is a blunt substitute. No cold-start metric or mitigation; a fresh container pull blocks the caller with no separate cold-start budget.

invoke does a single request() with no retry/backoff. Triggers are explicitly at-most-once (best-effort), the opposite of GCF event triggers which are at-least-once with retry policy and dead-letter. No retry config, no DLQ, no exponential backoff.

README states 'no separate meter' — billing is the deploy-time job bid (GB-seconds of the held cell), not per-invocation. GCF bills per invocation + compute time + egress. invoke() does not debit credits at all; a function holds a cell via heartbeats regardless of call count. The economic model is Cloud-Run-min-instance, not FaaS pay-per-call.

Function has no env field and no secrets. BidSpec is passed image/cmd/cpu/mem/duration/bid only. GCF supports --set-env-vars and Secret Manager mounts. No way to inject config or credentials into a handler.

No `ce-fn logs` command, no log streaming, no metrics (invocation count, latency, error rate), no tracing. CLI uses println! for status. README lists logs as a FaaS feature but none is implemented. InvokeResponse carries only output_hex/error, no stdout/stderr separation surfaced.

featureGaps.feature: HTTP/event trigger types beyond raw pubsub
featureGaps.notes: Only a single pubsub-topic trigger (`on`). GCF has HTTP triggers (public URL), Cloud Storage, Pub/Sub, Firestore, scheduler/cron, and Eventarc. No HTTP ingress, no cron trigger, no structured CloudEvents mapping (TriggerEvent is opaque hex).
Registry keys by name only; deploy replaces in place (insert overwrites). No revisions, no gradual rollout / traffic splitting, no rollback to a prior version, no aliases.

featureGaps.feature: Concurrency-aware request limits / payload size limits
featureGaps.notes: No max payload size on InvokeRequest (GCF caps request/response at 32MB for HTTP, 10MB for event). No per-function rate limiting or quota.

featureGaps.feature: Function update without redeploy / health checks
featureGaps.notes: No update-in-place of env/resources, no readiness/liveness probe, no graceful drain on kill.
`ls` shows static spec; no `describe`/`status` showing whether the cell is actually alive on the host, last-invoked, or job state. No reconciliation between registry and actual host job state.

robustnessIssues.issue: Trigger loop polls a bounded best-effort inbox ring (CeClient::messages) every 250ms; under load or any GC of the ring, events are silently dropped before the dedup window even sees them. messages_stream (SSE) exists in ce-rs and would be reliable, but run_trigger ignores it. Lost events = lost invocations with no record.

InvokeRequest payload has no size limit. A caller can submit an arbitrarily large payload; it is hex-encoded (2x blowup) into a single AppRequest and held entirely in memory on both ends. No bound on payload_hex length on decode either → DoS / OOM vector.

deploy() selects exactly one host and on failure has no fallback to the next-ranked candidate; if mesh_deploy errors (host went away between atlas read and deploy — a known TOCTOU), the whole deploy fails rather than retrying the next Candidate. select_host even comments on the atlas-changed race but does not handle it at deploy time.

kill() removes the function from the registry only after a successful mesh_kill, but if mesh_kill succeeds and remove() returns None (race), it errors 'vanished from registry' — and conversely a failed mesh_kill leaves a stale registry entry pointing at a dead/leaked cell with no reconciliation path. No way to force-forget a deployment whose host is unreachable.

Registry is a single JSON file written non-atomically (std::fs::write truncates then writes); a crash or two concurrent CLI processes mid-write corrupts it. No file lock, no temp-file+rename, no schema version field for migration.

WASM module_hash and host node-id strings are not validated as 64-hex anywhere in the deploy path; only the function name is validated. A malformed module_hash / host id is passed straight to the SDK, surfacing as an opaque downstream error. parse_node_id validates only for the grant audience.

select_host does an O(pool) sequential history() fan-out with per-host .unwrap_or(0) that silently treats any history fetch error as zero reputation — a transient API error on the strongest host's history could derank it. Also no timeout/parallelism; a slow node stalls placement.

encode() methods swallow serde errors via unwrap_or_default(), producing empty bytes on a (practically impossible but unhandled) serialization failure rather than surfacing an error; a downstream decode then fails with a confusing 'malformed' message.

Grant CLI uses default nonce=1 and Resource::Any with no warning; two grants minted with default args share a nonce (revocation collision) and Any-scoped tokens are broader than most users expect. No uniqueness check or guidance enforced.

Invoke with no cap and a host that requires authorization will fail remotely, but the client never sets a default/derived cap and the error path is entirely dependent on the (nonexistent) runtime — there is no client-side preflight that the caller is authorized.

testGaps.area: End-to-end invoke against a real or mock runtime (no serve-side exists, so the core path is wholly untested)
FnClient::deploy / deploy_on / invoke / kill / run_trigger — all network-touching client methods have zero tests (no mock CeClient; CeClient is concrete, not a trait, so it cannot be stubbed)
Trigger loop: dedup-under-replay, dropped-message handling, shutdown mid-event, malformed event payloads
Placement TOCTOU and deploy fallback to next candidate when chosen host fails
Registry concurrent/atomic write, corruption recovery, schema migration
Property tests for protocol round-trips (arbitrary bytes/payloads, large payloads, invalid hex) and placement ranking invariants (total order, stability)
Capability enforcement at the invoke boundary end-to-end (authorize_invoke is unit-tested in isolation but never wired to an invoke call)
CLI argument parsing / build_handler edge cases are only partially covered (mutually-exclusive flags, stdin payload, --select repetition) — no CLI integration test harness

docGaps: No serve-side runtime spec: the wire contract (ce-fn/invoke request/response semantics, error codes, capability enforcement point) is described in prose but there is no normative protocol doc or reference implementation for whoever builds the host daemon.
No examples/ directory and no runnable doctest (the lib.rs example is `no_run`). A FaaS product needs at least one end-to-end example handler (container + WASM) and a sample trigger producer.

README 'Status & limits' honestly admits the runtime is missing, but the top-of-README and Cargo description sell it as a working 'deploy/invoke/trigger' product — the missing data plane should be stated up front, not buried.

No architecture/sequence doc for the deploy→escrow→heartbeat→settle lifecycle, nor how invoke billing relates (or does not) to the held cell — the billing section contradicts a per-invocation FaaS mental model and needs an explicit 'this is Cloud-Run-style, not pay-per-call' callout.
No rustdoc on several public protocol fields' invariants (e.g. exit_code semantics, content_type usage by the runtime) and no documented size/limit constants.
No CONTRIBUTING/testing doc explaining that all tests are offline and what a real integration test would require (a running node + a runtime).
